content.text: Transparency Requirements

Transparency is the obligation to make information about an AI system available to those who are affected by it, oversee it, or regulate it. For agentic AI systems, transparency is especially challenging because agents act autonomously across multiple steps, call external tools, and produce outputs that may be hard to trace back to a specific decision point. Understanding who needs what information — and building the systems to provide it — is a core engineering concern.

Transparency requirements come from multiple sources: the EU AI Act, the NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 42001, GDPR, and emerging sector-specific regulations. The common thread is that different audiences — regulators, deployers, end users, and the public — have distinct legal entitlements and practical needs.

attrs.alt: Transparency Requirements — Four Audiences and Their Information Needs

content.text: The Four Transparency Audiences

Getting transparency right means engineering four separate information flows, not a single public disclosure. Conflating them leads to documents that are too technical for end users, too shallow for regulators, or too sensitive to publish publicly.
Audience
Legal Basis
Information Format
Typical Delivery
Regulator
EU AI Act Art. 11–13; national laws
Technical documentation; audit records
On-request to competent authority
Deployer (operator)
EU AI Act Art. 13; ISO 42001 A.8
System card; instructions for use
Contractual; API documentation
End user / affected person
EU AI Act Art. 13; GDPR Art. 13/22
Plain-language notice; explanations
UI disclosure; written notification
Public / researcher
Voluntary (model cards; EU GPAI)
Model card; safety evaluation
Public website; arXiv; registry
Regulator Transparency

The EU AI Act (Articles 11–12) requires High-Risk AI providers to maintain and make available on request a set of technical documentation sufficient for a competent authority to assess compliance. This is not a public document; it is produced for inspection.
General description: purpose, intended users, context of use
Architecture: high-level design, key components, interactions with other systems
Training methodology: data sources, labelling, pre-processing, validation/test split
Performance: accuracy, robustness, bias evaluation results with demographic breakdowns
Risk management documentation: risk register, mitigations, residual risks
Logging: how the system records inputs, outputs, and decisions for audit
Human oversight: how the system enables human review and intervention
Change history: version log documenting all material changes since conformity assessment

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class TechnicalDocumentation:
    """
    EU AI Act Article 11 — Technical documentation for High-Risk AI systems.
    Kept by the provider; produced on request to competent authorities.
    """
    system_name: str
    version: str
    intended_purpose: str
    intended_users: list[str]
    deployment_contexts: list[str]

    # Architecture
    architecture_description: str
    key_components: list[str]
    third_party_components: list[str]

    # Training
    training_data_sources: list[str]
    data_preprocessing: str
    validation_methodology: str

    # Performance
    accuracy_metrics: dict[str, float]       # e.g. {"precision": 0.91}
    bias_evaluation_results: dict[str, float] # e.g. {"demographic_parity_diff": 0.03}
    known_limitations: list[str]

    # Risk management
    risk_register_ref: str
    residual_risks: list[str]

    # Logging
    logging_mechanism: str
    log_retention_days: int

    # Human oversight
    oversight_mechanism: str

    # Change log
    changes: list[tuple[str, date, str]]  # (version, date, description)

    def document_complete(self) -> list[str]:
        """Return list of missing sections."""
        missing = []
        if not self.bias_evaluation_results:
            missing.append("bias_evaluation_results required for Art. 10 compliance")
        if not self.oversight_mechanism:
            missing.append("oversight_mechanism required for Art. 14 compliance")
        if not self.logging_mechanism:
            missing.append("logging_mechanism required for Art. 12 compliance")
        return missing
Deployer Transparency

Deployers are organisations that put a High-Risk AI system into use. The EU AI Act (Article 13) requires providers to give deployers instructions for use that are sufficient to allow them to understand the system's capabilities, limitations, and oversight requirements. ISO/IEC 42001 Annex A.8 adds the same obligation in its management system context.
System Cards

A system card (also called a model card for model-level disclosure) is the structured document that satisfies deployer transparency. It is typically a machine-readable JSON or YAML file alongside a human-readable summary.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class SystemCard:
    """
    Deployer-facing transparency document (EU AI Act Art. 13 / ISO 42001 A.8).
    Delivered to every deployer as part of API onboarding.
    """
    system_name: str
    version: str
    provider: str
    intended_purpose: str
    prohibited_uses: list[str]

    # Capabilities
    languages_supported: list[str]
    modalities: list[str]               # e.g. ["text", "code", "tool_calls"]
    max_context_length: int

    # Limitations
    known_failure_modes: list[str]
    performance_degradation_conditions: list[str]  # e.g. ["very long documents"]
    languages_with_degraded_performance: list[str]

    # Human oversight
    human_oversight_required: bool
    oversight_instructions: str         # what a human deployer must verify
    override_mechanism: str             # how a human can interrupt or correct the system

    # Data
    does_system_learn_from_user_data: bool
    data_retention_policy: str
    personal_data_processed: list[str]

    # Accuracy
    benchmark_results: dict[str, float]  # benchmark_name -> score

    # Contact
    incident_reporting_contact: str
    vulnerability_disclosure_url: str

    def to_instructions_for_use(self) -> str:
        """Generate a plain-English instructions-for-use document."""
        lines = [
            f"# Instructions for Use: {self.system_name} v{self.version}",
            f"Provider: {self.provider}",
            "",
            "## Intended Purpose",
            self.intended_purpose,
            "",
            "## Prohibited Uses",
        ]
        for use in self.prohibited_uses:
            lines.append(f"- {use}")
        lines += [
            "",
            "## Human Oversight",
            self.oversight_instructions,
            f"Override mechanism: {self.override_mechanism}",
        ]
        return "\n".join(lines)
End User Transparency

End users and affected individuals are often the people with the least technical literacy and the most at stake. The EU AI Act (Article 13(e)) requires that natural persons interacting with a chatbot or similar system be informed they are interacting with an AI — unless it is obvious from context. GDPR Article 22 adds a right to explanation for automated decisions with legal or similarly significant effects.
AI Disclosure at Runtime
For agentic systems deployed in customer-facing contexts, disclosure must be proactive — not buried in a terms of service page.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

DisclosureLevel = Literal["none", "brief", "detailed"]

@dataclass
class AIDisclosureConfig:
    """
    Runtime disclosure configuration for a deployed agentic system.
    The deployer sets this; the provider's instructions for use
    must specify the required minimum level.
    """
    system_name: str
    disclosure_level: DisclosureLevel
    disclosure_trigger: Literal["session_start", "first_message", "on_request"]
    include_provider_name: bool
    include_limitations: bool
    human_escalation_contact: str

BRIEF_DISCLOSURE_TEMPLATE = (
    "You are interacting with {system_name}, an AI assistant. "
    "It may make mistakes. A human agent is available at {contact}."
)

DETAILED_DISCLOSURE_TEMPLATE = (
    "You are interacting with {system_name}, an AI-powered assistant provided by {provider}. "
    "This system uses artificial intelligence to generate responses. "
    "Known limitations: {limitations}. "
    "If you believe you have received an incorrect response or wish to speak with a human, "
    "please contact {contact}."
)

def generate_disclosure(config: AIDisclosureConfig, provider: str, limitations: list[str]) -> str:
    if config.disclosure_level == "none":
        return ""
    if config.disclosure_level == "brief":
        return BRIEF_DISCLOSURE_TEMPLATE.format(
            system_name=config.system_name,
            contact=config.human_escalation_contact,
        )
    return DETAILED_DISCLOSURE_TEMPLATE.format(
        system_name=config.system_name,
        provider=provider if config.include_provider_name else "a third-party AI provider",
        limitations="; ".join(limitations[:3]) if config.include_limitations else "see documentation",
        contact=config.human_escalation_contact,
    )
Explainability for Automated Decisions

When an agent makes a decision that has a significant effect on a person — such as recommending credit denial or flagging a job applicant — GDPR Article 22 requires that the person can obtain a meaningful explanation. Generating this explanation must be built into the agentic pipeline, not retrofitted.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class DecisionExplanation:
    """
    GDPR Art. 22 compliant explanation for a significant automated decision.
    Must be generated at decision time — post-hoc reconstruction is unreliable.
    """
    decision_id: str
    decision_outcome: str               # e.g. "application not progressed"
    primary_factors: list[str]          # top factors that influenced the decision
    counterfactual: str                 # what would have led to a different outcome
    confidence: float                   # 0-1
    human_review_available: bool
    human_review_contact: str
    appeal_process: str

def generate_decision_explanation(
    decision_id: str,
    agent_reasoning: str,              # captured from the agent's output
    outcome: str,
    human_contact: str,
) -> DecisionExplanation:
    """
    Extract an explanation from the agent's reasoning trace.
    The reasoning trace must be captured at decision time.
    """
    # In production: use a structured extraction step to pull
    # primary_factors and counterfactual from the agent's CoT trace.
    return DecisionExplanation(
        decision_id=decision_id,
        decision_outcome=outcome,
        primary_factors=["extracted from reasoning trace"],
        counterfactual="extracted from reasoning trace",
        confidence=0.0,   # replace with calibrated confidence score
        human_review_available=True,
        human_review_contact=human_contact,
        appeal_process="Submit written request to appeals@company.com within 30 days",
    )
Public Transparency — Model Cards

Model cards are a research-community standard for public disclosure of AI model properties, popularised by the paper 'Model Cards for Model Reporting' (Mitchell et al., 2019). They are not legally required for most systems but are expected by enterprise customers, regulators, and the research community. The EU AI Act's GPAI provisions require public technical summaries that function similarly.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class ModelCard:
    """
    Public-facing model card — voluntary standard + EU GPAI technical summary requirement.
    Published on the provider's website and optionally submitted to model registries.
    """
    # Identity
    model_name: str
    model_version: str
    model_type: str              # e.g. "Large Language Model", "Multimodal Agent"
    architecture: str            # e.g. "Transformer decoder, 7B parameters"
    release_date: str
    license: str

    # Intended use
    primary_intended_uses: list[str]
    out_of_scope_uses: list[str]

    # Training
    training_data_summary: str
    training_data_cutoff: str
    known_data_biases: list[str]

    # Evaluation
    benchmark_results: dict[str, float]
    fairness_evaluation: dict[str, str]   # metric -> result description
    red_team_summary: str

    # Limitations
    technical_limitations: list[str]
    ethical_considerations: list[str]

    # Safety
    safety_evaluation_summary: str
    caveats_and_recommendations: list[str]

    # Contact
    contact: str

    def to_markdown(self) -> str:
        lines = [
            f"# Model Card: {self.model_name} ({self.model_version})",
            f"**Type:** {self.model_type} | **Architecture:** {self.architecture}",
            f"**Release:** {self.release_date} | **License:** {self.license}",
            "",
            "## Intended Use",
        ]
        for use in self.primary_intended_uses:
            lines.append(f"- {use}")
        lines += ["", "## Out-of-Scope Use"]
        for use in self.out_of_scope_uses:
            lines.append(f"- {use}")
        lines += ["", "## Training Data", self.training_data_summary, ""]
        lines += ["## Known Limitations"]
        for lim in self.technical_limitations:
            lines.append(f"- {lim}")
        return "\n".join(lines)
Logging and Audit Trails

Transparency to regulators and internal auditors depends on logs. The EU AI Act (Article 12) requires High-Risk AI systems to automatically generate logs that record the system's operation to the extent appropriate for the intended purpose. For agentic systems, this means logging at the agent level, not just the API level.

import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from datetime import datetime
from pathlib import Path

@dataclass
class AgentActionLog:
    """
    EU AI Act Art. 12 compliant log entry for one agent action.
    Written at action time; stored in append-only log.
    """
    log_id: str
    session_id: str
    timestamp: str           # ISO 8601
    action_type: str         # e.g. "tool_call", "llm_generation", "human_escalation"
    tool_name: str | None
    tool_args_hash: str | None    # hash of args, not raw args (may contain PII)
    tool_result_hash: str | None
    decision_summary: str         # plain-text summary of what the agent decided and why
    human_override: bool
    override_by: str | None

class AgentAuditLog:
    """Append-only structured log for agentic AI actions."""

    def __init__(self, log_path: Path):
        self._path = log_path
        log_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    def write(self, entry: AgentActionLog) -> None:
        with self._path.open("a") as f:
            f.write(json.dumps(asdict(entry)) + "\n")

    def entries_for_session(self, session_id: str) -> list[AgentActionLog]:
        entries = []
        if not self._path.exists():
            return entries
        with self._path.open() as f:
            for line in f:
                data = json.loads(line)
                if data["session_id"] == session_id:
                    entries.append(AgentActionLog(**data))
        return entries
Transparency vs Confidentiality Tensions

Transparency requirements can conflict with legitimate confidentiality interests. Providers may have trade secrets in their training methodology; deployers may have confidential business logic in their system prompts; logs may contain personal data that cannot be disclosed to third parties.
Conflict
Resolution Approach
Training data trade secrets vs. regulatory transparency
Describe methodology at process level; disclose data categories without revealing proprietary sources
System prompt confidentiality vs. user's right to know
Disclose that a system prompt exists and its general purpose; not required to reveal exact text
Personal data in logs vs. regulator access
Pseudonymise or anonymise logs; provide aggregate statistics rather than individual records where possible
Model weights as trade secrets vs. GPAI technical summary
EU Act requires technical summary, not model weights — architecture type and training data categories suffice
The EU AI Act does not require providers to reveal trade secrets. It requires enough information for competent authorities to assess compliance. These are different things.
Practical Transparency Implementation Checklist
Classify each deployment context into the applicable audience tiers (regulator, deployer, end user, public)
For High-Risk deployments: maintain technical documentation bundle (Art. 11) and logging infrastructure (Art. 12)
For all deployments with end users: implement AI disclosure at session start; provide human escalation path
For automated decisions with significant individual impact: implement decision explanation capture at generation time
For GPAI foundation models you build on: confirm your provider publishes a public technical summary
Draft system cards for all production agents deployed to third-party deployers
Establish a documented process for responding to regulatory data requests within applicable deadlines
Audit log retention: align with applicable regulation (EU AI Act requires retention for period of expected use)
Summary

Transparency requirements for agentic AI systems address four distinct audiences — regulators, deployers, end users, and the public — each with different entitlements and different information needs. Regulatory transparency centres on technical documentation and audit logs sufficient for compliance assessment. Deployer transparency is delivered through system cards and instructions for use that cover capabilities, limitations, and human oversight requirements. End user transparency requires runtime disclosure of AI involvement and, where decisions have significant individual effects, a meaningful explanation and human appeal path. Public transparency, while largely voluntary, is expected through model cards and safety evaluation summaries. Building these four information flows requires deliberate engineering choices at design time — logging, explanation capture, and structured documentation cannot be reliably retrofitted after deployment.
